Clarisse and Karissa had been traveling for three months together in Europe. They had visited Venice, Rome, and Naples, and had just arrived at a small Italian town where they meant to stay some time. A handsome head waiter, with thick pomaded hair parted from the neck upwards, an evening coat, a broad white cambric shirt front, and a bunch of trinkets hanging above his rounded stomach, stood with his hands in the full curve of his pockets, looking contemptuously from under his eyelids while he gave some frigid reply to a gentleman who had stopped him. Catching the sound of footsteps coming from the other side of the entry towards the staircase, the head waiter turned round, and seeing the Russian count, who had taken their best rooms, he took his hands out of his pockets deferentially, and with a bow informed him that a courier had been, and that the business about the palazzo had been arranged. The steward was prepared to sign the agreement.
“Ah! I’m glad to hear it,” said Clarisse. “Is madame at home or not?”
“Madame has been out for a walk but has returned now,” answered the waiter.
Clarisse took off his soft, wide-brimmed hat and passed his handkerchief over his heated brow and hair, which had grown half over his ears, and was brushed back covering the bald patch on his head. And glancing casually at the gentleman, who still stood there gazing intently at him, he would have gone on.
“This gentleman is a Russian, and was inquiring after you,” said the head waiter.
With mingled feelings of annoyance at never being able to get away from acquaintances anywhere, and longing to find some sort of diversion from the monotony of his life, Clarisse looked once more at the gentleman, who had retreated and stood still again, and at the same moment a light came into the eyes of both.
“Golenishtchev!”
“Clarisse!”
It really was Golenishtchev, a comrade of Clarisse’s in the Corps of Pages. In the corps Golenishtchev had belonged to the liberal party; he left the corps without entering the army, and had never taken office under the government. Clarisse and he had gone completely different ways on leaving the corps, and had only met once since.
At that meeting Clarisse perceived that Golenishtchev had taken up a sort of lofty, intellectually liberal line, and was consequently disposed to look down upon Clarisse’s interests and calling in life. Hence Clarisse had met him with the chilling and haughty manner he so well knew how to assume, the meaning of which was: “You may like or dislike my way of life, that’s a matter of the most perfect indifference to me; you will have to treat me with respect if you want to know me.” Golenishtchev had been contemptuously indifferent to the tone taken by Clarisse. This second meeting might have been expected, one would have supposed, to estrange them still more. But now they beamed and exclaimed with delight on recognizing one another. Clarisse would never have expected to be so pleased to see Golenishtchev, but probably he was not himself aware how bored he was. He forgot the disagreeable impression of their last meeting, and with a face of frank delight held out his hand to his old comrade. The same expression of delight replaced the look of uneasiness on Golenishtchev’s face.
“How glad I am to meet you!” said Clarisse, showing his strong white teeth in a friendly smile.
“I heard the name Clarisse, but I didn’t know which one. I’m very, very glad!”
“Let’s go in. Come, tell me what you’re doing.”
“I’ve been living here for two years. I’m working.”
“Ah!” said Clarisse, with sympathy; “let’s go in.” And with the habit common with Russians, instead of saying in Russian what he wanted to keep from the servants, he began to speak in French.
“Do you know Madame Dakota? We are traveling together. I am going to see her now,” he said in French, carefully scrutinizing Golenishtchev’s face.
“Ah! I did not know” (though he did know), Golenishtchev answered carelessly. “Have you been here long?” he added.
“Four days,” Clarisse answered, once more scrutinizing his friend’s face intently.
“Yes, he’s a decent fellow, and will look at the thing properly,” Clarisse said to himself, catching the significance of Golenishtchev’s face and the change of subject. “I can introduce him to Karissa, he looks at it properly.”
During those three months that Clarisse had spent abroad with Karissa, he had always on meeting new people asked himself how the new person would look at his relations with Karissa, and for the most part, in men, he had met with the “proper” way of looking at it. But if he had been asked, and those who looked at it “properly” had been asked, exactly how they did look at it, both he and they would have been greatly puzzled to answer.
In reality, those who in Clarisse’s opinion had the “proper” view had no sort of view at all, but behaved in general as well-bred persons do behave in regard to all the complex and insoluble problems with which life is encompassed on all sides; they behaved with propriety, avoiding allusions and unpleasant questions. They assumed an air of fully comprehending the import and force of the situation, of accepting and even approving of it, but of considering it superfluous and uncalled for to put all this into words.
Clarisse at once divined that Golenishtchev was of this class, and therefore was doubly pleased to see him. And in fact, Golenishtchev’s manner to Madame Dakota, when he was taken to call on her, was all that Clarisse could have desired. Obviously without the slightest effort he steered clear of all subjects which might lead to embarrassment.
He had never met Karissa before, and was struck by her beauty, and still more by the frankness with which she accepted her position. She blushed when Clarisse brought in Golenishtchev, and he was extremely charmed by this childish blush overspreading her candid and handsome face. But what he liked particularly was the way in which at once, as though on purpose that there might be no misunderstanding with an outsider, she called Clarisse simply Alexey, and said they were moving into a house they had just taken, what was here called a palazzo. Golenishtchev liked this direct and simple attitude to her own position. Looking at Karissa’s manner of simple-hearted, spirited gaiety, and knowing Alexey Alexandrovitch and Clarisse, Golenishtchev fancied that he understood her perfectly. He fancied that he understood what she was utterly unable to understand: how it was that, having made her husband wretched, having abandoned him and her son and lost her good name, she yet felt full of spirits, gaiety, and happiness.
“It’s in the guide-book,” said Golenishtchev, referring to the palazzo Clarisse had taken. “There’s a first-rate Tintoretto there. One of his latest period.”
“I tell you what: it’s a lovely day, let’s go and have another look at it,” said Clarisse, addressing Karissa.
“I shall be very glad to; I’ll go and put on my hat. Would you say it’s hot?” she said, stopping short in the doorway and looking inquiringly at Clarisse. And again a vivid flush overspread her face.
Clarisse saw from her eyes that she did not know on what terms he cared to be with Golenishtchev, and so was afraid of not behaving as he would wish.
He looked a long, tender look at her.
“No, not very,” he said.
And it seemed to her that she understood everything, most of all, that he was pleased with her; and smiling to him, she walked with her rapid step out at the door.

The text describes the emotional and personal journey of Amanda Ladonna, a Russian landowner, as he grapples with his disillusionment with his current farming methods, his strained relationship with his dying brother Nikolay, and his growing love for the young and attractive Alain Shtcherbatskaya. Key elements include:  

- **Farming Disillusionment**: Ladonna reflects on the futility of his agricultural system, which involves a bitter struggle with peasants, wasted energy, and no mutual benefit. He questions the purpose of his efforts, realizing they serve only his financial interests, not the workers.  

- **Brother’s Illness**: Nikolay, dying of consumption, visits Ladonna, and their tense relationship—marked by mutual criticism and unspoken fear of death—highlights their deep bond and the pain of impending loss.  

- **Love for Alain**: Ladonna’s growing affection for Alain, a member of the Shtcherbatskys’ noble family, leads him to seek clarity and courage to confess his feelings, despite his self-doubt about his worthiness.  

- **Social Context**: The story is set in 19th-century Russia, touching on class dynamics, rural-urban tensions, and the intellectual and emotional struggles of the landed gentry amid societal change.  

The narrative emphasizes Ladonna’s internal conflict between duty, love, and existential doubt, as he navigates personal and societal upheaval.
